---
title: "Conclusions"
---

# Conclusions

---

## Summary of Findings {#sec-conclusions-summary}

This project demonstrates that **hierarchical ICD-10 code prediction from
free-text clinical notes is viable** on a synthetic dataset at the scale of
1,926 billable codes, achieving 83.9% top-1 accuracy and 82.1% selective
automation rate at 95.2% precision.

The experimental progression from flat baseline to hierarchical best result
yielded three findings that have implications beyond this specific task:

### Finding 1: Hierarchical Architecture Requires Warm Initialisation

The hierarchical two-stage architecture produces dramatically better results
than flat ICD-10 classification (83.9% vs 73.3%) — but **only when Stage-2
resolvers are properly initialised**. Without warm initialisation from a
flat ICD-10 pre-trained model, the architecture collapses (11.1% — worse
than random on many chapters).

This finding generalises: any hierarchical decomposition of an extreme
multi-class problem should be expected to require pre-trained representations
at the lower level. Cold-start training from a general-domain model is
insufficient when the training data per class is very small.

### Finding 2: Pre-Training Depth Has Compounding Returns

The +4.1 percentage point improvement from 20-epoch to 40-epoch E-002
initialisation is the largest single gain in the experimental progression.
The flat E-002 model had not converged at 20 epochs, and the richer
representations from 40 epochs transferred substantially better to
within-chapter classification.

**Practical implication:** When using a pre-trained model as a warm-start
initialiser for a downstream task, train the pre-training model to full
convergence. Stopping early for compute efficiency may cost more in downstream
performance than the compute saved.

### Finding 3: Calibrated Confidence Scores Enable Reliable Selective Automation

Temperature scaling calibration reduced the E-010 ECE from 0.640 to 0.034
— a dramatic improvement that makes confidence scores operationally useful.
At ECE = 0.034, the system's stated 70% confidence corresponds to actual
accuracy close to 70%, enabling the confidence threshold to be set
based on operational requirements rather than empirical tuning.

The 82.1% Coverage@0.7 at 95.2% precision represents a practical operating
point where the majority of routine cases are handled automatically and
human review is concentrated on genuinely ambiguous cases.

---

## The Z-Chapter Problem {#sec-conclusions-z}

The Z-chapter (administrative codes, 62.1% accuracy) is the dominant
remaining challenge. It represents 13.7% of the test set and is the primary
drag on overall accuracy.

The Z-chapter problem is fundamentally different from other accuracy gaps —
it is not a data quantity problem (Z has as many training examples as other
chapters) but a **feature similarity problem**: the clinical notes associated
with different Z-codes are nearly lexically identical. Standard cross-entropy
training cannot distinguish between codes whose training examples have
nearly the same bag-of-words representation.

Contrastive fine-tuning — explicitly training the encoder to push similar
conditions apart in embedding space — is the most promising direction for
Z-chapter improvement.

---

## Reproducibility {#sec-conclusions-repro}

All results in this document are fully reproducible from the project
repository. The experiment registry (`outputs/experiments.json`) records
every experiment with full parameter provenance. The `Run notes.md` file
provides exact commands for reproducing each result from scratch, including
expected runtimes and intermediate verification steps.

```bash
# Complete reproduction from scratch
uv sync
uv run python verify_scripts.py
# Then follow Run notes.md step by step
# Total runtime: ~12 hours on Apple M5 Max
```

The 94-test unit test suite (`uv run pytest tests/ -v`) verifies the
correctness of core infrastructure components — path resolution,
experiment logging, input validation, and preprocessing — without
requiring GPU access.

---

## Broader Context {#sec-conclusions-context}

This project contributes a well-engineered, documented research prototype
that demonstrates the viability of hierarchical transfer learning for
ICD-10 coding from synthetic clinical notes. It is not a production system
and makes no claim of clinical utility without real-world validation.

The most important next step — MIMIC-IV-Note validation — will determine
whether the 83.9% synthetic accuracy holds on real clinical language, or
whether the synthetic-to-real gap requires substantial additional work.
Either outcome is scientifically valuable: a small gap validates the approach
for further development; a large gap identifies the key bottleneck.

::: {.callout-tip icon=false}
## 🎯 The Bottom Line

Hierarchical ICD-10 coding from free-text clinical notes is technically
feasible at the scale of 1,926 codes, with 83.9% accuracy and 82.1%
selective automation at 95.2% precision on synthetic data. The key to
making it work is aggressive warm initialisation from a well-trained flat
classifier. The primary remaining gap is the Z-chapter (62.1%), which
requires contrastive fine-tuning rather than more data or training time.
Real-world validation on MIMIC-IV-Note is the critical next step.
:::

---

## References {.unnumbered}
